straight_beat.name: Chet Pazulski
straight_beat.column: The Pirates lost yesterday, 7-6 to the Cubs, but let’s not pretend this game was a referendum on their season. They’re 9-6, a .594 winning percentage, and sitting at -1.5 games behind the NL Central lead. Yinzer math, boss. You take a team that’s won nine of its first 15 games, and you expect them to be in the thick of it

optimist.name: Franny Harsanyi
optimist.column:
The Pirates lost yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the fact that Paul Skenes is doing things that make me feel like I’ve stepped into a time machine. There he was, on the mound, throwing 102 pitches, and I almost called my daughter to come over and watch. That’s the feeling that comes back. That’s why we keep showing up.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Franny, the Pirates lost. How’s that a good thing?” Fair question. But here’s the thing—Skenes didn’t just throw 102 pitches. He pitched like a guy who’s been waiting for this moment his whole life. Steve Blass, Smoky Burgess, and now Skenes? That’s not a coincidence. It’s destiny. Blass was the ace of the ’70s, Burgess the heart of the ’60s, and Skenes? He’s got the same fire in his belly. The same grit. The same ability to make pitchers look like they’re throwing to a kids’ league.  

Sure, the Pirates didn’t win, but they didn’t fold. They fought. They batted around the order, they chased down leads, and they kept the game close. That’s the kind of grit that builds teams. And Skenes? He’s the glue. He’s the guy who makes you believe that even when the odds are against you, you can still make a run.  

Now, I know the standings aren’t pretty—9-6, three games behind. But here’s the thing: the Pirates are young. They’re loaded with talent. They’re not just a team with potential; they’re

pessimist.name: Wally Horvath
pessimist.column:
The Pirates lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to scream into a pillow. Yesterday’s 7-6 defeat to the Cubs wasn’t just another game; it was a masterclass in how the front office turns potential into frustration. You look at that roster—Skenes throwing gas, Burchett hitting like a man possessed, even that kid Blandino stealing bases like he’s got a rocket launcher—and you wonder, *what’s the plan here?*  

It’s not like the team’s talent is lacking. Skenes pitches the game of his life, and Nutting’s in a luxury box counting peanuts. The Pirates are a tax write-off with a roster attached. You’d think with a payroll that’s supposed to be competitive, you’d have a winning culture. Instead, you’ve got a lineup that’s full of holes, filled by the front office’s inability to make decisions. Why is there no depth in the infield? Why does the bullpen look like a middle school science fair?  

And let’s talk about the manager. You’d think with the talent on this team, you’d have a strategist who knows how to maximize it. But no, we’re stuck with someone who seems to think the only way to win is to hit home runs and hope the defense doesn’t screw up. The Cubs didn’t just beat us; they did it with precision, capitalizing on every mistake. That’s not a team with a plan—it’s a team with a panic button.  

The record’s 9-6, but that’s a lie. You don’t win 9 games out of 15 and feel good about it. You don’t watch a pitcher throw six innings of brilliance and think, *this is great*. You think, *why isn’t this translating into wins?* The front office has the money, the resources, the ability to make moves. Instead, they’re stuck in a loop of mediocrity,
